title: Knee Arthroscopy Diagnostic Sequence and Portals
summary: Systematic diagnostic arthroscopy of the knee: patellofemoral, medial, lateral, notch compartments. Key for meniscectomy, ACL, etc.
must_know: Standard portals: anterolateral (AL) viewing, anteromedial (AM) working; accessory for posterior or meniscus.

Diagnostic sequence: suprapatellar pouch, patellofemoral joint, medial gutter, medial compartment (MFC, MM, MTP), intercondylar notch (ACL/PCL), lateral compartment (LFC, LM, LTP), posteromedial/lateral if needed.
Structures at risk: saphenous nerve/vein (medial portals), peroneal nerve/lateral inferior genicular (lateral), popliteal structures (posterior).
Meniscus pathology: bucket handle, radial, horizontal, root tears - must probe and visualize fully before resection/repair.

structures_at_risk.structure: Infrapatellar saphenous branch / saphenous nerve
structures_at_risk.why_it_matters: Medial portal placement; neuroma or numbness risk.

structures_at_risk.structure: Popliteal neurovascular bundle
structures_at_risk.why_it_matters: Posterior compartment work or stray instruments.

landmarks.landmark: Patella, tibial tubercle, joint line, fibular head

safe_zones_or_intervals: AL portal: soft spot lateral to patellar tendon, 1cm above joint line.
AM portal: under arthroscopic visualization to avoid MFC and MM damage.
danger_zones: Transverse medial incision too posterior risks saphenous; lateral too distal risks peroneal.
procedure_specific_relevance: For meniscectomy: identify tear type, stability, and relation to popliteus or MCL before partial resection.
For ACL: assess notch, ACL footprint, associated meniscus/chondral injury.
common_pimp_questions: Describe the systematic diagnostic arthroscopy sequence of the knee.
What structures are at risk with anteromedial portal placement?
retrieval_phrases: knee arthroscopy portals diagnostic sequence meniscectomy
knee arthroscopy structures at risk saphenous peroneal
notes: Synthesized from existing v2_1 ACL/meniscus/rotator entries and standard OB sports knee knowledge in source files. Full portal anatomy and images would benefit from dedicated OB arthroscopy pages.

title: Shoulder Arthroscopy Diagnostic Sequence and Portals (for Rotator Cuff)
summary: Posterior viewing portal primary; systematic glenohumeral and subacromial diagnostic before cuff repair.
must_know: Posterior portal: 2cm inferior/medial to posterolateral acromion corner (soft spot).
Anterior portal: under visualization in rotator interval or subscapularis region.
Lateral (subacromial) portal for bursa and cuff work.
Diagnostic: biceps, labrum, rotator cuff undersurface, glenoid, humeral head, subacromial space (bursa, cuff bursal side, AC joint).

structures_at_risk.structure: Axillary nerve
structures_at_risk.why_it_matters: Inferior portals or excessive inferior retraction; deltoid function critical post-op for cuff.

structures_at_risk.structure: Suprascapular nerve
structures_at_risk.why_it_matters: Large/massive tears or releases at notch.

landmarks: Acromion, scapular spine, coracoid, AC joint, greater tuberosity.
safe_zones_or_intervals: Posterior portal safe in soft spot; stay lateral to avoid suprascapular.
danger_zones: Inferior glenohumeral work risks axillary; medial subacromial risks suprascapular.
procedure_specific_relevance: For rotator cuff: assess tear size, shape (U, L, crescent), retraction, tissue quality, and associated biceps/labral pathology before repair decision (debride, repair, or reconstruct).
common_pimp_questions: What is the primary viewing portal for shoulder arthroscopy and why?
Name three structures at risk during portal placement or subacromial decompression.
retrieval_phrases: shoulder arthroscopy portals diagnostic sequence rotator cuff
missing_needed_sources: Dedicated OB shoulder arthroscopy portal anatomy and systematic diagnostic checklist pages (current v2_1 rotator data is more open/mini-open focused).
notes: Based on existing rotator_cuff_repair entry in v2_1 and general shoulder anatomy from source. Thin on exact portal distances and images.

title: Anatomy Acdf
must_know: Anterior cervical: three fascial layers of neck important. Exposes C2-T1. Longus colli and anterior longitudinal ligament split; sympathetic chain on longus colli lateral to vertebral body.
Carotid sheath / recurrent laryngeal / sympathetic chain

Transverse skin crease incision at appropriate level (oblique from midline to posterior border of SCM). Split platysma, identify anterior SCM border, incise fascia, retract SCM lateral. Strap muscles medial. Carotid sheath lateral. Pretracheal fascia. Split longus colli/ALL subperiosteally to expose
Carotid sheath / recurrent laryngeal / sympathetic chain
Key steps and dangers of anterior cervical approach for ACDF (SCM, carotid, longus colli, sympathetic).
Indications for ACDF vs posterior approaches in cervical myelopathy/radiculopathy.

title: Anatomy Distal Radius Columns Watershed Listers
must_know: Distal radius responsible for 80% of axial load through wrist. Articulates with scaphoid via scaphoid fossa, lunate via lunate fossa, distal ulna via ulnar/sigmoid notch. Comprised of three columns: radial (radial styloid/scaphoid fossa; attachments for brachioradialis, long radiolunate, radioscapho

Lister's tubercle on dorsal surface ~1 cm proximal to articular surface; EPL tendon lies just ulnar to it before sharp turn into thumb. Important landmark to avoid EPL attritional rupture with long volar plate screws.

Watershed line: most volar and prominent aspect of distal radius (lunate portion most prominent with short radiolunate ligament attachment). Critical region to remain proximal to for volar plate fixation to avoid FPL rupture.
Internervous plane in FCR approach between FCR (median n.) and FPL (AIN).
Radial artery
Volar wrist capsule ligaments
FCR tendon sheath
Lister's tubercle
Radial styloid
Watershed line

Volar FCR approach: incision along FCR tendon sheath (ulnar/radial curve at crease), develop plane, retract FCR ulnarly, incise FCR sheath dorsally, retract FPL ulnarly, incise PQ borders and elevate. Proximal/distal extensions as needed for median n. or scaphoid.
Dorsal approach useful for displaced intra-articular dorsal lip fx or carpal work; no true internervous plane, between 3rd/4th extensor compartments.
Volar plating preferred; plate must not be placed distal to watershed line to avoid FPL irritation/rupture. Assess screw penetration with skyline and 23° elevated lateral views.

Distal radius responsible for 80% of axial load through wrist. Articulates with scaphoid via scaphoid fossa, lunate via lunate fossa, distal ulna via ulnar/sigmoid notch. Comprised of three columns: radial (radial styloid/scaphoid fossa; attachments

Watershed line: most volar and prominent aspect of distal radius (lunate portion most prominent with short radiolunate ligament attachment). Critical region to remain proximal to for volar plate fixation to avoid FPL rupture.
Internervous plane in FCR approach between FCR (median n.) and FPL (AIN).

title: Anatomy Hip Short External Rotators
must_know: Key intervals: posterior shares with Kocher-Langenbeck (gluteus maximus split, short ERs). Anterior: TFL/sartorius then rectus/gluteus medius. Lateral: no true internervous, splits abductors.

Direct lateral (Hardinge): split IT band, split gluteus medius/vastus lateralis (limit proximal split to 3-5cm above GT to protect superior gluteal n.). Detach anterior gluteus medius/minimus and vastus lateralis anteriorly.
Key intervals: posterior shares with Kocher-Langenbeck (gluteus maximus split, short ERs). Anterior: TFL/sartorius then rectus/gluteus medius. Lateral: no true internervous, splits abductors.

title: Anatomy Knee Extensor Mechanism
must_know: Superficial dissection develops medial skin flap to expose quadriceps tendon, medial border of the patella, and medial border of the patellar tendon. Medial parapatellar arthrotomy performed; infrapatellar fat pad is retracted or excised.

Deep dissection: patella dislocated and flipped laterally (or subluxated in variations); protect insertion of patellar tendon on tibia. Flex knee to 90 degrees to gain exposure to entire knee joint. Proximal extension incises between rectus femoris and vastus medialis and may split vastus intermediu

Blood supply to the anterior knee and patella is predominantly from the medial side (perforating arteries run in muscular fascia). Standard medial parapatellar with fat pad excision may jeopardize patellar circulation if combined with lateral release.
Superior lateral genicular artery
Skin (cutaneous blood supply / perforating arteries)
Midline of patella in line with tibial tubercle
Superior pole of the patella and tibial tubercle
Medial border of the patella and medial border of the patellar tendon
Patella (eversion / lateral flip or subluxation) and tibial tubercle (patellar tendon insertion)

Standard medial parapatellar: most common primary TKA approach. Midline longitudinal incision (straight or curved), begin 5 cm above superior pole of patella to level of tibial tubercle. Develop medial skin flap. Perform medial parapatellar arthrotomy between rectus femoris/VM and patella. Retract o

Advantages of standard medial parapatellar: familiar to most surgeons, provides excellent exposure even in challenging cases (obese, contractures, deformity). Blood supply generally medial-based. Incision planning: if multiple prior incisions choose more lateral; generally safe to cross previous tra

Variations: Midvastus - proximal arthrotomy extends into vastus medialis muscle belly (spares VMO insertion on quad tendon); patella subluxated laterally rather than fully everted; may allow accelerated rehab but less extensile and potential partial VMO denervation. Subvastus (Southern) - vastus med

title: Anatomy Proximal Humerus Neer Segments Circumflex
must_know: Proximal humerus: anatomic neck (old epiphyseal plate), surgical neck (weakened area). Four segments per Neer: greater tuberosity, lesser tuberosity, articular surface, shaft. Considered separate part if >1cm displacement or 45° angulation.

Deforming forces: pectoralis major displaces shaft anteriorly and medially; deltoid displaces proximal fragment laterally; supraspinatus/infraspinatus/teres minor externally rotate greater tuberosity; subscapularis internally rotates articular segment or lesser tuberosity.

Blood supply: anterior humeral circumflex artery (anterolateral ascending branch, arcuate artery terminal and main supply to greater tuberosity); posterior humeral circumflex artery (recent studies suggest main blood supply to humeral head).
Cephalic vein
Deltopectoral interval / cephalic vein
Greater and lesser tuberosities, surgical neck

Deltopectoral (anterior): standard for many 3/4-part and arthroplasty. Interval between deltoid (axillary n.) and pectoralis major (medial/lateral pectoral n.). Cephalic vein in interval. Release subscapularis for exposure (protect axillary n. distally/medially). Can extend distally to humerus.
Lateral deltoid split: for proximal humerus ORIF and rotator cuff. Limit split to protect axillary nerve (3-5cm below acromion).

Proximal humerus: anatomic neck (old epiphyseal plate), surgical neck (weakened area). Four segments per Neer: greater tuberosity, lesser tuberosity, articular surface, shaft. Considered separate part if >1cm displacement or 45° angulation.

Deforming forces: pectoralis major displaces shaft anteriorly and medially; deltoid displaces proximal fragment laterally; supraspinatus/infraspinatus/teres minor externally rotate greater tuberosity; subscapularis internally rotates articular segmen

Musculocutaneous nerve

summary: Module for achilles_tendon_repair (created during procedure readiness pass).

structures_at_risk.structure: Achilles: confluence of gastroc/soleus. Paratenon provides blood supply and gliding. Sural nerve runs lateral to tendon in distal leg.
Sural nerve
Sural nerve

landmarks.landmark: Posteromedial incision (catalog ID) for open repair. Protect paratenon. Krackow or similar locking suture. Early protected weightbearing protocols common.

title: Anatomy Bimalleolar Ankle Orif
summary: Module for bimalleolar_ankle_orif (created during procedure readiness pass).
must_know: Per map evidence: OB describes specific approaches (e.g. lateral for bimalleolar) but catalog gaps prevent full recommended IDs. No free-form guessing; limited to available (e.g. posteromedial for some ankle variants).
Key approach for this case per OB and map?
Modified hinge joint: tibia, fibula, talus. Tibial plafond and talus broader anteriorly and wider laterally.
Deltoid: superficial resists eversion (navicular/sustentaculum/talus insertion); deep resists ER of talus (primary anterolateral talar restraint).
Lateral ligament complex: ATFL (primary restraint to anterior/IR/inversion of talus, most frequently injured); CFL (deep to peroneals, 2nd most common); PTFL (strongest of lateral complex).
Syndesmosis: AITFL (Chaput tubercle tibia to Wagstaffe fibula), PITFL (strongest, Volkmann fragment to posterior lateral malleolus), IOL (distal continuation of interosseous membrane), ITL.

Approaches from 1047: direct lateral (fibula ORIF + syndesm), posterolateral (posterior fibula + posterior malleolus), posteromedial (medial malleolus + posterior malleolus), direct medial (medial malleolus ORIF).
Tendons at risk: peroneals (posterolateral fibula plating), posterior tibial tendon (posteromedial medial malleolus screws).

NV at risk: anterior tibial artery + deep peroneal nerve (anterior approach between EDL/EHL); posterior tibial artery + tibial nerve (posteromedial between FDL/FHL); superficial peroneal nerve (crosses fibula ~distal 1/3); sural nerve (posterolateral).

structures_at_risk.structure: Primary structure at risk?

Syndesmosis: AITFL (Chaput tubercle tibia to Wagstaffe fibula), PITFL (strongest, Volkmann fragment to posterior lateral malleolus), IOL (distal continuation of interosseous membrane), ITL.

Approaches from 1047: direct lateral (fibula ORIF + syndesm), posterolateral (posterior fibula + posterior malleolus), posteromedial (medial malleolus + posterior malleolus), direct medial (medial malleolus ORIF).
Tendons at risk: peroneals (posterolateral fibula plating), posterior tibial tendon (posteromedial medial malleolus screws).

Lateral ligament complex: ATFL (primary restraint to anterior/IR/inversion of talus, most frequently injured); CFL (deep to peroneals, 2nd most common); PTFL (strongest of lateral complex).
Syndesmosis: AITFL (Chaput tubercle tibia to Wagstaffe fibula), PITFL (strongest, Volkmann fragment to posterior lateral malleolus), IOL (distal continuation of interosseous membrane), ITL.

Approaches from 1047: direct lateral (fibula ORIF + syndesm), posterolateral (posterior fibula + posterior malleolus), posteromedial (medial malleolus + posterior malleolus), direct medial (medial malleolus ORIF).
Tendons at risk: peroneals (posterolateral fibula plating), posterior tibial tendon (posteromedial medial malleolus screws).

NV at risk: anterior tibial artery + deep peroneal nerve (anterior approach between EDL/EHL); posterior tibial artery + tibial nerve (posteromedial between FDL/FHL); superficial peroneal nerve (crosses fibula ~distal 1/3); sural nerve (posterolateral).
common_pimp_questions: What nerve crosses the fibula at the distal 1/3 and is at risk in the direct lateral approach?
Which is the strongest syndesmotic ligament and which fragment does it attach to?

title: Anatomy Clavicle Fracture Orif
summary: Module for clavicle_fracture_orif (created during procedure readiness pass).
must_know: Per map evidence: OB describes specific approaches (e.g. lateral for bimalleolar) but catalog gaps prevent full recommended IDs. No free-form guessing; limited to available (e.g. posteromedial for some ankle variants).
Key approach for this case per OB and map?
ORIF indicated for displaced midshaft with shortening >2cm, comminution, open injury, floating shoulder, symptomatic nonunion, or high-demand patients/athletes.
Direct superior or anterior approach over clavicle; careful subcutaneous flaps.
Supraclavicular nerves (C3-4 branches) cross the field; injury causes numbness over anterior chest wall and shoulder.
Deforming forces: SCM pulls medial fragment superiorly/posteriorly; deltoid origin and trapezius weight pull lateral fragment inferiorly/anteriorly.
Subclavian vessels and brachial plexus at risk with marked displacement or excessive medial retraction/dissection.

title: Anatomy Lisfranc Orif
summary: Module for lisfranc_orif (created during procedure readiness pass).
must_know: Lisfranc (tarsometatarsal) joint complex: medial, middle, lateral columns. Instability >2mm shift indication for operative. Bony vs purely ligamentous patterns affect ORIF vs primary arthrodesis choice.

Primary arthrodesis (1st-3rd TMT): for purely ligamentous, delayed, chronic, or complete dislocations (Type A/C2). Denude cartilage, screws/plates +/- graft. Equivalent or superior function in ligamentous cases per level 1 evidence.
ORIF vs primary arthrodesis indications for Lisfranc (bony vs ligamentous).
Lisfranc (TMT) complex: medial column (1st TMT + medial cuneiform), middle (2nd/3rd TMT + cuneiforms), lateral (4th/5th TMT + cuboid).
Operative threshold typically >2mm displacement on weightbearing or stress views. Bony vs ligamentous injury affects ORIF vs primary fusion choice.
Dual longitudinal incisions (1-2 web space for medial column) between EHL/EHB for 1st TMT exposure.
Reduction sequence: stabilize intercuneiform (medial column base) first, then 1st-3rd TMT (transarticular screws or bridge plates), lateral column as needed.
Primary arthrodesis preferred for purely ligamentous, chronic, or highly displaced patterns (denude, rigid fixation).
structures_at_risk: Lisfranc (TMT) complex: medial column (1st TMT + medial cuneiform), middle (2nd/3rd TMT + cuneiforms), lateral (4th/5th TMT + cuboid).
Operative threshold typically >2mm displacement on weightbearing or stress views. Bony vs ligamentous injury affects ORIF vs primary fusion choice.
Dual longitudinal incisions (1-2 web space for medial column) between EHL/EHB for 1st TMT exposure.
Reduction sequence: stabilize intercuneiform (medial column base) first, then 1st-3rd TMT (transarticular screws or bridge plates), lateral column as needed.
Primary arthrodesis preferred for purely ligamentous, chronic, or highly displaced patterns (denude, rigid fixation).

landmarks.landmark: ORIF: single or dual longitudinal incisions (web space 1-2 for medial column). Expose first TMT between EHL/EHB. Reduce intercuneiform first, then fix 1st-3rd TMT with transarticular screws (or plates for comminution). Anatomic reduction required.
landmarks.source_section: Technique (Open reduction and rigid internal fixation)

landmarks.landmark: Key steps and reduction sequence in Lisfranc ORIF via longitudinal incisions.

title: Anatomy Olecranon Fracture Orif
summary: Module for olecranon_fracture_orif (created during procedure readiness pass).
must_know: Per map evidence: OB describes specific approaches (e.g. lateral for bimalleolar) but catalog gaps prevent full recommended IDs. No free-form guessing; limited to available (e.g. posteromedial for some ankle variants).
Key approach for this case per OB and map?
Osteology: olecranon + coronoid form the greater sigmoid (semilunar) notch articulating with trochlea; provides flexion-extension and adds to elbow stability.
Triceps inserts posterior proximal ulna (blends with periosteum, radial nerve C7); anconeus on lateral olecranon (radial C7).

ORIF techniques: tension band (transverse, no comminution) converts triceps distraction to compression (K-wires must engage anterior cortex but risk AIN and limit forearm rotation); dorsal plate preferred for comminuted, Monteggia, or oblique fractures extending distal to coronoid; IMN can be combined with tension band.
Excision + triceps advancement: salvage for elderly, osteoporotic, <50% joint involvement, or nonunion; leads to decreased extension strength and potential instability if ligamentous injury missed.

Posterior approach details: midline incision curving around olecranon; fully identify/protect ulnar nerve (palpable 2cm proximal to medial epicondyle, use penrose); V- or chevron osteotomy 2cm from tip after pre-drilling/tapping; subperiosteal exposure of distal humerus after retracting olecranon fragment.

title: Anatomy Pilon Ankle Fracture Orif
summary: Module for pilon_ankle_fracture_orif (created during procedure readiness pass).
must_know: Per map evidence: OB describes specific approaches (e.g. lateral for bimalleolar) but catalog gaps prevent full recommended IDs. No free-form guessing; limited to available (e.g. posteromedial for some ankle variants).
Key approach for this case per OB and map?
Modified hinge joint: tibia, fibula, talus. Tibial plafond and talus broader anteriorly and wider laterally.
Deltoid: superficial resists eversion (navicular/sustentaculum/talus insertion); deep resists ER of talus (primary anterolateral talar restraint).
Lateral ligament complex: ATFL (primary restraint to anterior/IR/inversion of talus, most frequently injured); CFL (deep to peroneals, 2nd most common); PTFL (strongest of lateral complex).
Syndesmosis: AITFL (Chaput tubercle tibia to Wagstaffe fibula), PITFL (strongest, Volkmann fragment to posterior lateral malleolus), IOL (distal continuation of interosseous membrane), ITL.
CT often changes surgical plan for posterior malleolus morphology and impaction.

title: Reduction Scfe Pinning
summary: Module for scfe_pinning (created during procedure readiness pass).

structures_at_risk.structure: SCFE: unstable growth plate (physis) from shear overload. Epiphysis slips posteroinferiorly. Contralateral pinning for high risk (age <10, obese male, endocrine).
Femoral head blood supply / AVN risk

Percutaneous in situ fixation (stable/unstable): single cannulated screw (or 2 for unstable) perpendicular or slightly oblique to physis from anterior proximal femur. Advance until 5 threads cross physis ( <5 increases progression risk). Screw tip 5m

Modified Dunn (surgical hip dislocation, open realignment): for severe/unstable slips needing correction. Ganz technique lateral decubitus, straight lateral incision, gluteus maximus split, trochanteric flip, protect blood supply (retinacular vessels
structures_at_risk.source_section: Techniques (Surgical hip dislocation, open capital realignment)

must_know: Per map evidence: OB describes specific approaches (e.g. lateral for bimalleolar) but catalog gaps prevent full recommended IDs. No free-form guessing; limited to available (e.g. posteromedial for some ankle variants).
Key approach for this case per OB and map?
Modified hinge joint: tibia, fibula, talus. Tibial plafond and talus broader anteriorly and wider laterally.
Deltoid: superficial resists eversion (navicular/sustentaculum/talus insertion); deep resists ER of talus (primary anterolateral talar restraint).
Lateral ligament complex: ATFL (primary restraint to anterior/IR/inversion of talus, most frequently injured); CFL (deep to peroneals, 2nd most common); PTFL (strongest of lateral complex).
Syndesmosis: AITFL (Chaput tubercle tibia to Wagstaffe fibula), PITFL (strongest, Volkmann fragment to posterior lateral malleolus), IOL (distal continuation of interosseous membrane), ITL.

Approaches from 1047: direct lateral (fibula ORIF + syndesm), posterolateral (posterior fibula + posterior malleolus), posteromedial (medial malleolus + posterior malleolus), direct medial (medial malleolus ORIF).
Tendons at risk: peroneals (posterolateral fibula plating), posterior tibial tendon (posteromedial medial malleolus screws).

NV at risk: anterior tibial artery + deep peroneal nerve (anterior approach between EDL/EHL); posterior tibial artery + tibial nerve (posteromedial between FDL/FHL); superficial peroneal nerve (crosses fibula ~distal 1/3); sural nerve (posterolateral).

Approaches from 1047: direct lateral (fibula ORIF + syndesm), posterolateral (posterior fibula + posterior malleolus), posteromedial (medial malleolus + posterior malleolus), direct medial (medial malleolus ORIF).
Tendons at risk: peroneals (posterolateral fibula plating), posterior tibial tendon (posteromedial medial malleolus screws).

NV at risk: anterior tibial artery + deep peroneal nerve (anterior approach between EDL/EHL); posterior tibial artery + tibial nerve (posteromedial between FDL/FHL); superficial peroneal nerve (crosses fibula ~distal 1/3); sural nerve (posterolateral).
landmarks: Modified hinge joint: tibia, fibula, talus. Tibial plafond and talus broader anteriorly and wider laterally.
Deltoid: superficial resists eversion (navicular/sustentaculum/talus insertion); deep resists ER of talus (primary anterolateral talar restraint).
Lateral ligament complex: ATFL (primary restraint to anterior/IR/inversion of talus, most frequently injured); CFL (deep to peroneals, 2nd most common); PTFL (strongest of lateral complex).
Syndesmosis: AITFL (Chaput tubercle tibia to Wagstaffe fibula), PITFL (strongest, Volkmann fragment to posterior lateral malleolus), IOL (distal continuation of interosseous membrane), ITL.

Tendons at risk: peroneals (posterolateral fibula plating), posterior tibial tendon (posteromedial medial malleolus screws).

NV at risk: anterior tibial artery + deep peroneal nerve (anterior approach between EDL/EHL); posterior tibial artery + tibial nerve (posteromedial between FDL/FHL); superficial peroneal nerve (crosses fibula ~distal 1/3); sural nerve (posterolateral).
